mappings.rationaleAI: History from manual abacus and mechanical calculators through vacuum-tube, transistor, and integrated-circuit machines directly covers calculating devices including the abacus and mechanical calculators (B). Digital and analog architectures correspond to types of computers (D.1). Coverage of microprocessors and mobile devices addresses applications of microcomputers, minicomputers, and supercomputers (D.4). Information theory connections address communication and information theory (A).

Bell's 1876 patent, evolution from point-to-point to public switched telephone networks, cellular and VoIP variants directly correspond to telephone and telecommunications systems and equipment (I.4).

Historical progression from optical and electric telegraphs through telephone, radio, television, and internet covers telephone and telecommunications systems (I.4) and telegraph systems (I.3). Mention of transition from voice to data and network architecture connects to communications satellite systems (I.7).

Commercial AM/FM radio broadcasting from the 1920s directly matches radio communications systems (I.5). Terrestrial television and cable television broadcasting correspond to television communications systems (I.6).

Design and operation of devices using transistors, diodes, and integrated circuits directly addresses electronic components and techniques (K). Active components including transistors, semiconductor devices correspond to K.1.a. Coverage spans analog and digital circuits and PCB fabrication (K.1).
Development from daguerreotype through SLR and digital sensors directly addresses still photography (G.1). Motion picture camera types correspond to motion-picture and television photography (G.2).

Neural networks, deep learning, natural language processing, perception, and decision-making directly cover developments in artificial intelligence including expert systems, natural-language processing, computer vision, and robotics (D.5).

Portable wireless telephone operating over cellular radio-frequency networks, with evolution from Motorola's 1973 demonstration through 5G, directly addresses telephone and telecommunications systems and equipment (I.4).

Formalization of algorithm and computation via the Turing machine and contributions to stored-program computer design relate to programming systems and the concept of software (D.2). Cryptanalysis of the Enigma cipher at Bletchley Park directly addresses encryption and decryption techniques and history of cryptology (I.9).

Shannon's information theory quantifying information in bits and covering encoding, transmission, storage, and compression directly addresses communication and information theory (A). Discussion of discrete digital signals and continuous analogue forms aligns with analog and digital forms of information (H.1.a).

Organization, circulation, reference services, and computerized catalogs in library networks directly address organization and retrieval including bibliographic databases and computerized catalogs (H.1.c). Libraries as educational and reference systems correspond to H.2.c.

Mass reproduction of text from woodblock printing through Gutenberg's press to digital printing directly addresses printing machines and processes (E.3). Letterpress, offset lithography, and gravure correspond to production of printing plates (E.4). Woodblock printing and lithography specifically match mechanical techniques (E.4.a).

Television as a telecommunication medium for mass broadcast directly covers television communications systems and equipment (I.6). Digital transmission and display technologies (CRT, plasma, LCD, OLED) relate to optical systems for audio and video (F.3). Smart TVs integrating internet functions connect to display and dissemination via television monitors and interactive terminals (H.1.d).

Encryption, authentication, classical ciphers through modern symmetric-key and public-key systems, and history of cryptology directly match encryption and decryption techniques and devices including signal security, message authentication, and history of cryptology (I.9).

Radar using pulsed or continuous radio waves for detection of distance, direction, and velocity, including air traffic control and military applications, directly addresses radar systems and equipment (J.1).

Capture of images via chemical film and electronic sensors, covering amateur, commercial, art, and scientific applications, directly addresses the technology of photography (G) and still photography (G.1).

Information disseminated through print, postal systems, broadcasting, and digital media, including newspapers, directly addresses book, newspaper, and magazine publishing (I.1). Historical mention of postal systems as a distribution channel connects to postal systems and equipment (I.2).
Making content publicly available in print or digital form, centered on books, newspapers, and magazines, directly addresses book, newspaper, and magazine publishing (I.1).

Books as written works distributed in printed, audio, or electronic form, with formats evolving from clay tablets and scrolls to codex manuscripts, directly address book, newspaper, and magazine publishing (I.1). Historical progression from clay tablets connects to simple recording implements and devices (E.1).

The global TCP/IP network interconnecting systems and hosting the World Wide Web, email, and streaming directly addresses electronic networks (I.8). Online database access and web-based information services connect to public information utilities and on-line database search systems (H.2.b).

Miniaturized assembly of transistors, resistors, and capacitors on semiconductor substrate, from SSI through VLSI and SoC, directly matches integrated circuits as miniature arrays of interconnected active or passive circuit elements including microprocessors (K.1.c).
Coherent monochromatic light through stimulated emission, with applications in optical disc storage and fiber-optic communications, directly addresses holography and laser photography (G.3).
Edison's invention of the phonograph directly addresses mechanical systems for sound recording and reproducing — phonographs (F.1).

Inventor of the European movable-type printing press using oil-based ink and durable alloy, producing the Gutenberg Bible as the first mechanically printed book in Europe, directly addresses printing machines and processes (E.3).
Design, construction, and operation of robots integrating AI, sensing, and manipulation directly relates to developments in artificial intelligence including robotics (D.5).

Use of electromagnetic waves for wireless transmission including broadcasting, two-way communication, and international frequency allocation directly addresses radio communications systems and equipment (I.5).

Transmission and creation of meaning via coded messages through channels, covering historical evolution driven by writing, print, broadcast, and digital technologies, broadly addresses communication and information theory (A) and major systems of communication (I).

The shift to information-technology-centred economies initiated by the transistor, internet, and World Wide Web relates to applications of computers (D.4). Moore's Law, data storage, and computing economics connect to information processing and systems (H).

Electronic interactive systems played on arcade, console, computer, mobile, and cloud platforms represent a major application domain for microcomputers and personal computing, connecting to applications of microcomputers, minicomputers, and supercomputers (D.4).
